FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying smartphone and social media variables: texting (B=0.92, 95% CI 0.29-1.55), video chat (B=0.72, 95% CI 0.09-1.36), video games (B=0.42, 95% CI 0.06-0.78), social networking. Applies B1—separate β reported for each SM modality.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses testing SM → BMI percentile outcome (Table 2). Each screen time variable examined in separate regression models with B coefficients and 95% CIs.

FT_IC4.evidence: Beta coefficients and 95% CIs reported in Table 2 for all screen time modalities (total screen time B=0.22, texting B=0.92, video chat B=0.72, video games B=0.42, social networking B=0.67, all with p-values)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis. Prospective cohort design with baseline screen time exposure and 1-year follow-up BMI percentile outcome.

FT_IC6.evidence: Original published paper, no duplicate detected.

FT_EC7_reason: B18 carve-out applies. Screen time (smartphone/SM) is the independent variable (IV) predicting a non-SM outcome (BMI percentile). FT-EC7 requires SM as dependent variable with only demographic predictors. Here SM is IV → outcome.
notes: Strong inclusion candidate. All IC criteria met. Multiple qualifying contemporary screen modalities (texting, video chat, social networking, video games) with separate coefficients. Prospective design, large diverse US-ABCD sample.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study. Part 1: N=11,727 (weekday screen time, ages 9-10.92 years). Part 2: N=11,672 (weekend screen time, ages 9-10.92 years).

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen time modalities measured separately with regression models: television, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, video chat, social media. Applies B1—separate β/correlation coefficients reported for each screen time type.

FT_IC3.evidence: Correlation and regression analyses. Multiple linear regression models testing screen time → outcome relationships (mental health, academic performance, sleep, peer relationships). Pearson correlations and regression coefficients reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Correlation coefficients (r values) and standardized regression coefficients (β) with p-values reported throughout. Results presented for weekday and weekend screen time separately.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis. Cross-sectional baseline assessment (October 2018) examining relationships between screen time and multiple outcomes (mental health, academics, sleep, peer relationships).

FT_IC6.evidence: Original published paper, no duplicate detected.

FT_EC7_reason: B18 carve-out applies. Screen time is the independent variable predicting multiple non-SM outcomes (depression, anxiety, academic performance, sleep, peer relationships). FT-EC7 requires SM as DV with demographic-only predictors.
notes: Strong inclusion candidate. All IC criteria met. Multiple contemporary screen modalities with separate coefficients (B1). Large diverse ABCD sample. Cross-sectional design examining screen time as predictor of mental health, academic, and social outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study subsample substudy. N=67 participants (ages 11-12 years; 31/67 female, 23/67 White). Four geographically dispersed ABCD sites. Passive sensing data collected 2019-2020.

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone variables measured objectively via EARS app: streaming (mean 1h 57 min/day), communication/texting (48 min/day), social media (36 min/day), gaming (41 min/day), video chat implied in communication category. Applies B1—separate app category use reported.

FT_IC3.evidence: No inferential test of SM ↔ non-SM outcome. Paper reports descriptive statistics (means, SDs), correlations between self-report and objective sensing data (r=0.49), and ANOVA for demographic differences in app use. This is a measurement validation/feasibility substudy. Applies B12 carve-out: SM-vs-objective concordance only, no SM → outcome analysis.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric data reported: means and SDs for app use times (Table 1), Pearson correlation (r=0.49) between self-report and objective data, ANOVA F-values and p-values.

FT_IC5.evidence: Applies B12 + B14 carve-out. This is a measurement paper comparing self-reported vs objective smartphone assessment. No original analysis of SM predicting external outcomes. No SM ↔ non-SM outcome coefficient. Feasibility/acceptability focus only.

FT_IC6.evidence: Original published paper, no duplicate detected.

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable. Paper fails FT-IC5 (measurement substudy without external outcome analysis). Does not meet threshold for full-text screening inclusion.
notes: EXCLUDE. Fails FT-IC5. This is a substudy validating passive sensing methodology against self-report in a small subsample (N=67). No analysis of smartphone/SM predicting external outcomes. Measurement paper comparing objective vs self-reported screen time concordance (r=0.49). Does not qualify as original empirical analysis predicting non-SM outcomes.
